title: Berlin Natural History Museum - Quick Summary
items: **The Berlin Natural History Museum (Museum für Naturkunde) holds the world's tallest mounted dinosaur.** The Giraffatitan skeleton stands 13.27 metres high in the glass-roofed central hall. It is a genuine wow moment, and the single best reason to come.

**Two more headline exhibits share the building.** Tristan Otto is a real Tyrannosaurus rex, one of the only original T. rex skeletons in Europe, and the Berlin Archaeopteryx is often called the most valuable fossil in the world, the missing link between dinosaurs and birds.

**Tickets in 2026: 11 euros adult, 5 euros reduced, free under 6.** A family ticket (2 adults plus up to 3 children under 14) is 18 euros. Book a timed slot on the museum website in advance on weekends and school holidays to skip the entrance queue.
**Closed Mondays. Open Tuesday to Sunday until 18:00**, with last entry 30 minutes before. It is a calm, well-lit, one-to-two-hour visit that works brilliantly on a rainy afternoon or with kids.

**The old free first Sunday is gone.** Berlin's Museumssonntag scheme ended in December 2024 and is not running in 2026, so ignore older blogs promising free entry. There is no monthly free day here now.

**Getting there: U-Bahn Naturkundemuseum on the U6, a 300 metre walk.** The museum sits on Invalidenstraße just north of the government quarter, close to Hauptbahnhof and Nordbahnhof, so it pairs easily with a Berlin Wall or central sightseeing day.
